reasoning: The 30-day window for the first date (April 19, 2026) captures a massive, nationwide wave of fuel protests that occurred between April 8-11, 2026, involving blockades in at least four major locations (Dublin, Cork, Galway, Limerick) over several days. ACLED's methodology of coding separate locations and days as distinct events means these fuel protests alone will likely add 10-20 events to the monthly total. Added to a standard baseline of ~10-12 events from ongoing anti-immigration and Gaza protests, the total will almost certainly exceed the 360-day average of 16.083. For the later dates, the probability drops significantly because the fuel protests ended around April 11-12. However, the probability remains slightly above 0.5 for the near-to-mid term (May to October 2026) because the overall structural trend of protests in Ireland is rising (fueled by recent anti-asylum and geopolitical protests), meaning the recent instantaneous rate is likely higher than the trailing 360-day average. Over the long-term horizons (2027-2036), secular trends become unpredictable and probabilities revert gracefully to the 0.5 maximum uncertainty prior.
